coredata.dc:title: Rapid pH-responsive self-disintegrating nanoassemblies balance tumor accumulation and penetration for enhanced anti-breast cancer therapy

coredata.dc:description:
The dilemma of tumor accumulation and deep penetration has always been a barrier in antitumor therapy. Stimuli-responsive size changeable drug delivery systems provide possible solutions. Nevertheless, the low size-shrinkage efficiency limited the antitumor effects. In this study, an instant pH-responsive size shrinkable nanoassemblies named self-aggregated DOX@HA-CD (SA-DOX@HA-CD) was formulated using small-sized hyaluronic acid modified carbon dots (HA-CD) as monomers, which could self-aggregate into raspberry-like structure via hydrophobicity force in neutral pH and rapidly disassemble into shotgun-like DOX-loaded CD monomer in simulated tumor microenvironment (pH 6.5), owing to the transformation in electrical charge and hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity of this system. The transmission electron microscopy showed that the clustered SA-DOX@HA-CD had a diameter of ~150 nm, and thoroughly disassembled into ~30 nm nanoparticles in response to acidic environment. The disassemble efficiency was approximately 100%. Attributed to this property, SA-DOX@HA-CD led to enhanced cellular internalization and accumulation in 4T1 cells in simulated tumor microenvironment, as well as deep tumor penetration in 3D tumor spheroid model. Besides, the imine bond between DOX and HA-CD endowed DOX with pH-responsive release profile in the acidic lysosome environment. Furthermore, in the orthotopic 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model, SA-DOX@HA-CD demonstrated higher tumor accumulation than non-aggregated DOX-HA-CD. Meanwhile, in response to the acid tumor microenvironment, the dissociated DOX-HA achieved deep tumor penetration, which consequently resulted in 2.5-fold higher antitumor efficiency. The formulation of self-aggregated SA-DOX@HA-CD provides a simple and effective alternative to prepare pH-responsive size-shrinkable nanodrug delivery systems.
Statement of significance The heterogeneity of tumor vasculature and the high tumor interstitial pressure lead to the barriers in tumor accumulation and deep penetration, which calls for opposite properties (e.g. size) of drug delivery systems. To address this dilemma, various size changeable nanoparticles have been developed utilizing special features of tumor microenvironment, such as pH, enzyme and reactive oxygen species. Nevertheless, the current strategies face the problems of incomplete hydrolysis of chemical bonds or insufficient enzyme degradation, which result in only partial size shrinkage, hindering the tumor deep penetration effects. Here we developed a self-assembled nanocluster, which could respond to acidic pH rapidly and thoroughly disassemble into small nanodots due to the alteration of hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity/charge, leading to approximately 100% dissociation. This strategy provides a new concept for design of size changeable drug delivery systems.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis schematic diagram of SA-DOX@HA-CD.
(A) 1H NMR of citric acid, PEI, HA, and HA-CD. (B) 1H NMR of HA-CD-p-CBA and DOX@HA-CD. (C) FT-IR spectra of HA-CD, DOX@HA-CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD.

(A) Size and morphology of DOX@HA-CD. Scale bar = 100 nm. (B) Size and morphology of SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 7.4. Scale bar = 500 nm. (C) Size and morphology of SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 6.5. Scale bar = 100 nm. (D) The UV absorbance of HA-CD, DOX and DOX@HA-CD.

(A) Size of SA-DOX@HA-CD in pH 7.4 (red) and in pH 6.5 (green). (B) Size of SA-DOX@HA-CD at different time points in pH 6.5. (C) Size of SA-DOX@HA-CD in different pH. (D) Zeta potential of SA-DOX@HA-CD in different pH. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) Cellular uptake in 4T1 cells analysed by FACS (n = 3, mean ± SD), *** p < 0.001, n.s: non-significant. (B) Cellular uptake in 4T1 cells analysed by CLSM. Scale bar = 100 µm.

(A) Dynamic cellular internalization in 4T1 cells incubated with DOX@HA-CD, SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH7.4 and SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH6.5. Blue: nucleus, green: doxorubicin. Scale bar = 20 µm. (B)-(C) The co-localization of DOX and lysosome in 4T1 cells after treated with free DOX, DOX@HA-CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH7.4 or pH6.5. Blue: nucleus, green: doxorubicin, red: lysosome. Scale bar = 10 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Tumour spheroids penetration after incubation with free DOX, DOX@HA-CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 7.4 or pH 6.5. The concentration of DOX was 10 µg·mL−1. Green represented DOX. Scale bar = 100 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The images of ex vivo tumours of mice at 1 h, 4 h, 8 h and 24 h after i.v injection. (B) The average fluorescence intensity of ex vivo tumours of mice after injection (n=3, mean ± SD), ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001. (C) The frozen tumour sections of ex vivo tumours at 24 h after injection. Scale bar = 50 µm.

(A) Tumour volume changes of after intravenous injection in orthotopic 4T1 tumour-bearing mice. (B) The tumour weights from each group on the 15th day. ** p < 0.01 and *** p < 0.001, respectively. (C) Tumour images isolated from mice treated with DOX, DOX@HA-CD or SA-DOX@HA-CD. (D) Changes of body weight of orthotopic 4T1 tumour-bearing mice after treatment. (E) Immunohistochemistry staining of ki-67 and Caspase-3 of tumor sections. Scale bar = 200 µm.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The dilemma of tumor accumulation and deep penetration has always been a barrier in antitumor therapy. Stimuli-responsive size changeable drug delivery systems provide possible solutions. Nevertheless, the low size-shrinkage efficiency limited the antitumor effects. In this study, an instant pH-responsive size shrinkable nanoassemblies named self-aggregated DOX@HA-CD (SA-DOX@HA-CD) was formulated using small-sized hyaluronic acid modified carbon dots (HA-CD) as monomers, which could self-aggregate into raspberry-like structure via hydrophobicity force in neutral pH and rapidly disassemble into shotgun-like DOX-loaded CD monomer in simulated tumor microenvironment (pH 6.5), owing to the transformation in electrical charge and hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity of this system. The transmission electron microscopy showed that the clustered SA-DOX@HA-CD had a diameter of ~150 nm, and thoroughly disassembled into ~30 nm nanoparticles in response to acidic environment. The disassemble efficiency was approximately 100%. Attributed to this property, SA-DOX@HA-CD led to enhanced cellular internalization and accumulation in 4T1 cells in simulated tumor microenvironment, as well as deep tumor penetration in 3D tumor spheroid model. Besides, the imine bond between DOX and HA-CD endowed DOX with pH-responsive release profile in the acidic lysosome environment. Furthermore, in the orthotopic 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model, SA-DOX@HA-CD demonstrated higher tumor accumulation than non-aggregated DOX-HA-CD. Meanwhile, in response to the acid tumor microenvironment, the dissociated DOX-HA achieved deep tumor penetration, which consequently resulted in 2.5-fold higher antitumor efficiency. The formulation of self-aggregated SA-DOX@HA-CD provides a simple and effective alternative to prepare pH-responsive size-shrinkable nanodrug delivery systems.

The heterogeneity of tumor vasculature and the high tumor interstitial pressure lead to the barriers in tumor accumulation and deep penetration, which calls for opposite properties (e.g. size) of drug delivery systems. To address this dilemma, various size changeable nanoparticles have been developed utilizing special features of tumor microenvironment, such as pH, enzyme and reactive oxygen species. Nevertheless, the current strategies face the problems of incomplete hydrolysis of chemical bonds or insufficient enzyme degradation, which result in only partial size shrinkage, hindering the tumor deep penetration effects. Here we developed a self-assembled nanocluster, which could respond to acidic pH rapidly and thoroughly disassemble into small nanodots due to the alteration of hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity/charge, leading to approximately 100% dissociation. This strategy provides a new concept for design of size changeable drug delivery systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles have exhibited great potential in tumor targeting. A number of nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems have significantly elevated the tumor targeted accumulation of chemotherapy in some tumor types utilizing the enhance permeation and retention effect (EPR effect) [1,2]. Nevertheless, the dense matrix formed with stromal cells and extracellular matrix (ECM) as well as high interstitial fluid pressure (IFP) not only shape the tumor microenvironment (TME), but also construct physical barriers for the deep penetration of chemotherapy, which has been reckoned as the main reason for chemotherapy failure [3–5]. The size, shape and surface modification of nanoparticles influence the distribution behavior in tumor. Specifically, nanoparticles less than 30 nm in diameter usually distribute in the deeper region in tumor compared with nanoparticles over 100 nm. Moreover, spherical particles outweighed linear polymers in tumor penetration [6–8]. However, small nanoparticles also face the challenge of rapid clearance and short blood circulation. Besides, the poor tumor retention severely impaired the therapeutic effects. As a result, the dilemma of high tumor retention and deep penetration represents a great challenge in nanoparticle design.

To tackle this problem, bio-responsive size changeable nanoparticles are developed. The lower pH and enzyme-rich microenvironment provides optimal stimulus for smart transformable drug delivery system design. Various stimuli-triggered size shrinkable drug delivery systems (such as pH-, matrix metalloproteinase- (MMP), hypxia- or even oxidation-responsive) have been developed [9–12]. The rapid change of particle size from over 100 nm to 10-40 nm significantly facilitated the deep penetration of antitumor drugs, demonstrating good balance between prolonged blood circulation and tumor deep penetration [13].

Nevertheless, the current strategies still face problems of insufficient degradation of core nanoparticle or disintegration of chemical bonds, which weakened the penetration efficiency of small particles. MMP-triggered degradation of gelatin NPs usually takes 24 to 48 h in vitro [14]. For MMP substrate peptide conjugated nanoparticles, though change in particle size is observed after 1 h incubation with MMP, over 80% nanoparticles remained intact [15,16]. Acetal bond, imine bond and hydrozone bond are widely applied in the construction of pH-responsive nanoparticles. The protonation of these groups leads to reversal of charge or hydrophobicity in response to acidic pH, leading to rapid size shrinkage [17]. Unfortunately, in these micellar systems, the disassemble of larger nanoparticles usually results in linear polymers, rather than spherical particles, which hampered the deep penetration efficiency [18].

Carbon dots (CDs) have become a hot spot in drug delivery for their potential application in drug delivery and imaging for the small size (< 10 nm) and unique properties [19,20], which can be conjugated or encapsulated to larger nanoparticles forming a clustered structure via acid-cleavable or enzyme-degradable bond. After responding to the stimulus, the CDs are disintegrated and subsequently penetrate into deeper region in tumor [21–23]. These smart bio-responsive NPs provide solutions for addressing the dilemma of tumor accumulation and deep penetration. In our previous work, we have constructed a hyaluronic acid modified carbon dot by using HA and branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) as carbon source and functional materials (HA-CD-DOX), which enhanced active tumor targeting and facilitated deep penetration [24]. PEI is endowed with strong proton buffering capacity and therefore has been extensively investigated as gene delivery system. The protonation of amine groups in slightly acidic pH will lead to disruption of hydrophobic/hydrophilic equilibrium of the material [25,26]. Enlightened by the pH-responsiveness of protonable groups, here we further modified the amino group DOX@HA-CD with di-tert-butyl dicarbonate (Boc-anhydride) to formulate a pH-sensitive self-disintegratable nanocluster, which could self-aggregated into raspberry-like nanoclusters with diameters of ~100 nm in neutral pH and rapidly disassemble into small DOX-loaded carbon dots in pH 6.5, accelerating the tumor deep penetration. The disintegration of large NP primarily relies on the repulsion of each individual monomer due to the transition of charge and hydrophobicity, rather than the hydrolysis of chemical bond, which is a more instant and thorough response, leading to completely disintegration into small particles. More importantly, after disintegration, the obtained small nanoparticles remained in spherical form, which is beneficial to tumor deep penetration. To the best of our knowledge, this manuscript is the first report of pH-responsive self- disassembled raspberry-like nanoclusters. In the present manuscript, we formulated and verified the rapid pH-responsiveness of self-aggregated DOX@HA-CD (SA-DOX@HA-CD), and explored the deep penetration capacity and antitumor activity of this system in a breast cancer model.

Branched polyethylenimine (Mw =1,800) and citric acid (Mw = 192.14) were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Corporation. Hyaluronic acid (HA, MW = 3,000-10,000) was obtained from Freda Biopharm Co. di-tert-butyl dicarbonate was obtained from SinoPharm (Beijing, China). EDC (1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride) and NHS (N-hydroxyl succinimide) were obtained from J & K Scientific (Beijing, China). Doxorubicin (DOX) was obtained from Meilun Biotechnology (Dalian, China). p-CBA (4-carboxybenzaladehyde) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade.

4T1 cells (murine breast carcinoma cells) were purchased from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China) and cultured with RPMI 1640 medium (Gibco, Thermal Fisher, USA) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco, Thermal Fisher, USA), streptomycin and penicillin (100 U/mL) in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37°C. The medium was refreshed every 1-2 days during cell culture.

BALB/c mice were obtained from Dossy Experimental Animals Co., Ltd. (Certificate No. SCXK2015-030, Chengdu, China). Mice were housed in pathogen-free, temperature-controlled facility. All animal experiments were approved by the experiment animal administrative committee of Sichuan University and performed under the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of self-assembled DOX@HA-CD (SA-DOX@HA-CD)
Synthesis of carbon dots with branched PEI, hyaluronic acid and citric acid
ce:section.ce:para: HA-modified carbon dots (HA-CDs) was prepared as follows. Citric acid (200 mg) and HA (120 mg) were dissolved in deionized water, and were added to 400 mg branched-polyethylene mine (PEI) dissolved in water under magnetic stirring to form mixture. Then, the mixture was transferred into autoclave vessel for heating at 180°C for 60 min in a furnace. Finally, when the temperature cooled down to room temperature, the resulting crude products were purified by vacuum filtration and ultrafiltration (MWCO = 50,000) to yield the brown aqueous product HA-CDs.

HA-CD-p-CBA was synthesized according to a previous method. Briefly, 20 mg 4-carboxybenzaladehyde (p-CBA), 25.47 mg EDC and 15.33 mg NHS (molar ratio, 1:1:1) were dissolved in 2 mL dichloromethane and incubated for 4 hours under stirring at RT until the system became transparent. After removing dichloromethane by rotary evaporation, the product was re-dissolved in 4 mL DMSO, and 40 mg HA-CDs dissolved in 1mL deionized water was added dropwise. Then, after reaction for 6 hours, the mixture was dialyzed for three times against deionized water (MWCO = 3,500) and concentrated to ~5 mL by ultrafiltration (MWCO = 30,000). Finally, the HA-CD-p-CBA dispersion were dialyzed against DMSO (MWCO = 3,500) to allow further reaction.

Doxorubicin hydrochloride (20 mg) and triethylamine (molar ratio 1:3) were dissolved in 2 mL anhydrous DMSO for reaction about 12 hours to obtain hydrophobic DOX, which was added to HA-CD-p-CBA for reaction for 24 h. Finally, 43 mL deionized water was added to the mixture followed by ultrafiltration (MWCO = 30,000) until the effluent was colourless to obtain the product DOX@HA-CD. DOX loading of DOX@HA-CD was determined by ultraviolet spectrophotometry by quantification of doxorubicin in ultrafiltration effluent (Shimadzu UV-240 spectrophotometer).

DOX@HA-CD was dispersed in deionized water. First, DOX@HA-CD (DOX concentration 400 µg·mL−1) was diluted with methanol (1:4, v/v), followed by addition of 6.45 nM Boc-anhydride/methanol solution dropwise under magnetic stirring. After incubation for 4h, methanol was removed by rotary evaporation and the mixture was dialyzed (MWCO = 3,500) against deionized water for 3 times. Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) was conducted on Magna IR-750 (Nicolet Instrument, USA). The 1H NMR spectrum was recorded by Varian UNITY INOVA400 (USA). The element analysis was performed by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS Supra, United Kingdom). The morphology of the DOX@HA-CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). The size was measured by ZS90 Zetasizer dynamic light scattering detector (Malvern Instruments, UK).

In this system, by adjusting the amount of Boc-anhydride, differences in aggregation degree occurred. Therefore, to screen the optimal particle size, different amount of Boc-anhydride (2.58, 3.44, 4.35, 5.16, 6.45 nM in methanol) was introduced to each aliquot (2.4 mg) of DOX@HA-CD dispersion. After dialysis with deionized water and adjust the pH to 7.4, the particle size was measured by ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, UK).

In the pH responsiveness assay of SA-DOX@HA-CD, the pH of buffer was adjusted to the range between 4.4 to 7.4, and the particle size and zeta potential of each sample was measured by ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, UK). To verify the swiftness of the pH-sensitive disintegration, SA-DOX@HA-CD was incubated in pH 6.5, and the particle size at different time points (5 min, 10 min, 15 min) were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-sensitive drug release behaviour of SA-DOX@HA-CD
ce:section.ce:para: SA-DOX@HA-CD (doxorubicin 100 µg·mL−1, 3 mL) or free doxorubicin was put into dialysis bag (MWCO = 8,000). After tightly packed, the dialysis bag was immersed into 50 mL PBS containing 0.2% Tween-80 at different pH (pH 7.4, pH 6.5 and pH 5.5) and were incubated in an incubator under 37°C at 75 rpm for 48 h. The samples were obtained at predetermined time points to analyse the release profile of DOX. The concentration of DOX in each sample was determined by fluorescence spectrophotometer at wavelength Ex = 488 nm/Em = 555 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro stability of SA-DOX@HA-CD in serum
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the stability of SA-DOX@HA-CD, the changes in turbidity in buffer containing 10% or 50% FBS was measured. SA-DOX@HA-CD dissolved in HEPES buffer at different concentrations (40 µg·mL−1, 20 µg·mL−1 and 10 µg·mL−1) was mixed with equal volume of FBS. After incubation for 0 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h and 24 h at 37°C with gentle shaking, the transmittance at 750 nm was measured by a microplate reader (Varioskan, Thermal Fisher, USA). HEPES buffer containing 10% or 50% FBS was used as blank control.

The hemocompatibility assay was conducted to assess the biocompatibility of SA-DOX@HA-CD [27]. Blood was collected from mice by retro-puncture. Red blood cells (RBC) were obtained by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min. Then RBC was washed and diluted with PBS to obtain a final concentration of 2% (v/v), which was further incubated with equal volume of SA-DOX@HA-CD (40 µg·mL−1, 20 µg·mL−1, 10 µg·mL−1, 5 µg·mL−1 and 1 µg·mL−1) at 37°C with gentle shaking. Samples were collected at 1 h, 2 h and 4 h. And the absorbance of haemoglobin in were measured at 540 nm with a microplate reader (Varioskan, Thermo Fisher, USA). PBS and RBCs treated with water or PBS were used as positive or negative control, respectively.

To analyse the cellular uptake of SA-DOX@HA-CD at physical pH or simulated acidic TME, Breast cancer cells (4T1 cells) were seeded on sterile glass coverslip in a six-well plate and cultured with RPMI-1640 containing 10% FBS at a density of 5 × 105 cells per well. After incubation for 24 h, the media were replaced with 2 mL serum-free RPMI-1640 culture media at pH 7.4 or 6.5. DOX@HA-CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD dispersion were added at DOX concentration of 5 µg·mL−1. After further incubation for 6 h, cells were rinsed 3 times with ice-cold PBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min at room temperature. Then cells were stained with DAPI solution (0.5 µg·mL−1) for 10 min. After further rinse with PBS, the cellular internalization was observed with a laser scanning confocal microscope (CLSM, Zeiss LSM 800, Germany). All observations parameters were kept constant during the observation.

The quantitative cellular uptake efficiency was further measured by flow cytometry. 4T1 cells were seeded in six-well plate at a density of 1 × 106 cells per well. The following procedures were identical with CLSM analysis. After incubation with different formulations for 6 h, cells were washed with PBS, trypsinized and re-suspended in 0.4 mL PBS. The fluorescent intensity of cells was measured by FACS (FACS Celesta, BD, USA).

To study the intracellular transportation behavior of SA-DOX@HA-CD, we explored the dynamic endocytosis process on 4T1 cells. DOX@HA-CD and free DOX were added and incubated with cells at pH 7.4, while SA-DOX@HA-CD was incubated at pH 7.4 and pH 6.5. At predetermined time points (1 h, 2 h and 4 h), treated cells were washed with ice-cold PBS, fixed and stained with DAPI. After washing, the fluorescence images were acquired by CLSM. Besides, to explore the subcellular distribution of SA-DOX@HA-CD, cells were stained with Lyso-tracker dissolved in serum-free RPMI 1640 culture medium (100 nM) after 4 h incubation with different formulations. After staining, cells were rinsed by cold PBS, fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde and stained with DAPI. The co-localization of nanoparticles with endo-lysosome was observed by CLSM (Zeiss LSM 800, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro tumour spheroid penetration assay
ce:section.ce:para: Sterilized 2% (w/v) low gelling temperature agarose gel was pre-coated in 96-well plates (70 µL per well). 4T1 cell suspension was seeded (1 × 103 cells per well) and cultured for 3-5 days under standard incubation conditions. Then compact and homogeneous tumour spheroids were screened out and treated with free DOX, DOX@HA-CD, SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 6.5 and SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 7.4 with equivalent DOX dose of 10 µg·mL−1. After 6 h co-incubation under cultivation conditions, the tumour spheroids were washed several times with cold PBS and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 30 min. Finally, different depths fluorescence images of tumor spheroids were obtained by CLSM (Zeiss LSM 800, Germany).

The quantitative deep penetration was also determined by flow cytometry. After treatment with different formulations for 6 h, the tumor spheroids were treated with Accumax Cell Dissociation Solution to obtain single cell suspension. Cells were than washed with PBS for 5 times to exclude the uninternalized particles and subjected to flow cytometry analysis (FACS Celesta, BD, USA).

The MTT method was utilized to test the cytotoxicity of SA-DOX@HA-CD. 4T1 cells were seeded in 96-well plates and cultured overnight. Then, the cell medium was replaced with serum-free medium with pH adjusted to either 7.4 or 6.5 containing the same DOX concentrations of each preparation. After another 24 h incubation, 100 µL MTT solution (1000 µg·mL−1) was added into each well and incubated for 4 h at 37°C. Next, 100 µL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added into each well to dissolve the formazan. Finally, the absorbance was measured with Varioskan Flash (Thermo Scientific, USA) at wavelength of 490 nm. Cell viability was calculated with following formula: cell viability (%) = Atest-Ablank/Acontrol-Ablank × 100%, where Atest and Aconrol represent the absorbance of cells treated with different test solutions and blank culture medium, respectively. Ablank represent the absorbance of well without cells. Each sample was conducted with 5 parallel wells. Besides, the apoptosis of tumor cells were determined by flow cytometry after Annexin-IV and PI staining (FACS Celesta, BD, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of orthotopic breast cancer model
ce:section.ce:para: Female BALB/c mice (6-8-week old,18-22 g) were employed to establish the tumour-bearing mice model in this study. 4T1 cell suspension (1 × 107/ml in PBS, 0.1 mL) was injected to the right second mammary fatty pad of each mouse. After tumour volumes reached about 50-100 mm3, the tumour-bearing mice were divided into 4 groups randomly: PBS, free DOX, DOX@HA-CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging and tissue distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The orthotopical tumour-bearing mice were divided into four groups as previously described. Each group were injected intravenously with free DOX or DOX containing formulation (DOX dose 3 mg·kg−1). At predetermined 4 h and 24 h after injection, mice were sacrificed under anesthesia. After heart perfusion with PBS and 4% paraformaldehyde, the major organ and tumour were collected for live imaging by the IVIS® Spectrum System (Perkin Elmer, USA). To compare the deep penetration of different DOX containing formulations, the tumors were further embedded into paraffin and subjected to sections. After staining with DAPI, the sections were imaged by CLSM (Zeiss LSM 800, Germany) to observe the distribution of DOX in tumor tissues.

The orthotopical 4T1 tumour-bearing mice were assigned into four groups randomly when the tumours reached about 50-100 mm3 as previously described. Each mouse was intravenously given free DOX, DOX@HA-CD or SA-DOX@HA-CD at DOX dose of 3 mg·kg−1 every other day for 5 total injections since day 6. From the first day of administration, the body weight and tumour volume were measured and recorded every two days. On day 21 after tumour inoculation, mice were sacrificed under anesthesia to collect main organs and tumours. The tumor volume was calculated according to the following formula: volume (mm3) = 1/2 A (length) × B (width)2. The tumours were weighted and then sectioned for haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and immunohistochemical staining with ki-67 and caspase-3. The main organs were collected for sections and H&E staining for pathological observation.

The systematic safety of free DOX and SA-DOX@HA-CD were evaluated by blood biochemistry assay. Healthy BALB/c mice (sex in half, 20 ± 2 g) were randomly assigned into three groups and treated with PBS, DOX, or SA-DOX@HA-CD intravenously at DOX dose of 4 mg·kg−1 every two days for 5 total times. Then, blood samples were collected by orbital puncture to obtain the plasma samples by centrifuging blood samples at 5000 rpm for 10 min. Then serum aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine (CRE) and creatine kinase (CK) levels were analyzed according to the instruction of manufacturers (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, China).

Statistical comparisons were performed by one-way ANOVA with Tukey post-hoc test. Significant differences were indicated by *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001, respectively. Data were presented as mean ± SD. Experiments were performed at in triplicate unless otherwise noted.

The corresponding functional groups were identified by FT-IR spectral methods (Fig. 1C and Fig. S1). The structure of HA-CD-p-CBA was confirmed by the peaks at 1634 and 1678 cm−1 which represented the amide and aldehyde stretching vibration of νC=O confirming the conjugation of p-CBA and HA-CD. Furthermore, DOX was conjugated to HA-CD-p-CBA via alamide reaction, which was proved by the peaks at 1623 and 1554 cm−1 representing the stretching vibration peak of νC=N and/or the amide correlation peak. In the spectrum of SA-DOX@HA-CD, the tert-butyl and ester group peaks could be observed due to the introduction of tert-butoxycarbonyl (BOC). The methyl stretching vibration of tert-butyl was observed that peaks centred at 2948.40 and 2832.39 cm−1. Tert-butyl was further identified by two characteristic split peaks located at 1418.27 cm−1 and 1365.64 cm−1. An ester bond also was distinguished by two characteristic stretching vibration peaks lied in 1164.64 cm−1 and 1073.14 cm−1. These results demonstrated that BOC was conjugated to DOX@HA-CD.

The morphology of DOX@HA-CD, SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 7.4 and SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 6.5 were characterized by TEM and DLS. The DOX@HA-CD NPs were sub-spherical in morphology and well-dispersed with an average diameter of ~30 nm (Fig. 2 A). For SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 7.4, irregular morphology was observed and the diameter is ~150 nm (Fig. 2B). While at pH 6.5, the nanocluster disassembled into small nanoparticles (~40 nm, Fig. 2C). The hydrodynamic diameter was measured by DLS techniques were consistent with the TEM observation.

UV/vis spectroscopy analysis was conducted to characterize HA-CD and DOX@HA-CD (Fig. 2D). Two characteristic bands assigned to aromatic C=C -* transition (~240 nm) and C=O n-* transition (~340 nm) were observed in the UV/vis absorption spectrum of HA-CD. The peak at 500 nm was attributed to characteristic absorption of DOX in DOX@HA-CD, proving the successful conjugation of DOX and HA-CD. Besides, to analyse whether the fluorescence of HA-CD would affect the detection of DOX, we scanned the excitation and emission wavelength of HA-CD. The results indicated that HA-CD did not interfere the detection of DOX (Fig. S2).

In this system, DOX@HA-CD was endowed with amphiphilic property after modification with BOC anhydride, and self-aggregated to form SA-DOX@HA-CD nanoclusters. Different proportions of hydrophobic group may result in different amphiphilic properties, ultimately leading to the differences in particle size and morphology. In the present manuscript, the optimal particle size was ~165 nm when adding 6.45 nM BOC dilution (Fig. S3).

Since amino-rich PEI was used to prepare HA-CD, SA-DOX@HA-CD was positively charged (~10 mV at pH 7.4). In acidic environment, its positive potential would further increase due to the protonation of the residual amino group, which may trigger the dissociation of the aggregates into the initial monomer. As shown in Fig. 3 A, an aggregated state with particle size about 150-200 nm was observed at pH 7.4, while at pH 6.5, the nanocluster disassembled into the BOC modified DOX@HA-CD monomer with diameter of 30-40 nm. Notably, the size change in different pH is rather swift. As shown in Fig. 3B, the size of SA-DOX@HA-CD shrunk rapidly from 165.53 ± 6.34 nm to 37.12 ± 4.40 nm within 5 min's incubation at pH 6.5. Besides, the change in size and zeta potential at different pH was also monitored. For particle size, a turning point was observed at ~pH 6.8, where the particle size rapidly changed from over 100 nm to ~30 nm. And the particle diameter remained stable below pH 6.8 (Fig. 3C). Due to the protonation of residual amino group on PEI, the zeta potential increased with the decrease of pH, which may facilitate the interaction between negatively charged cell membrane and positively charged NPs (Fig. 3D).

In previous reports, pH sensitive size shrinkable nanoparticles have exhibited great potential for the design of tumor targeted drug delivery systems. pH-labile chemical bonds including amide bond, ortho ester, Schiff base bond, etc [29–31]. The protonation of certain functional groups may lead to reversal of charge or hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity [32,33]. One example is poly (β-amino esters), which contains protonable tertiary amine groups [34,35]. Rui et al. formulated a poly (β-amino esters) derivative for the efficient delivery of CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing system [36]. Here we conjugated di-tert-butyl decarbonate with the amine group on HA-CD, thereby introducing hydrophobic group in the DOX@HA-CD nanoparticle. At neutral pH, each of the small NP (DOX@HA-CD) could self-assemble into a larger nanocluster with the help of hydrophobic force. While at acidic pH, due to the protonation of amine groups remained on the surface of HA-CD, the charge and pH disassemble of nanocluster into small NPs. As shown in Fig. 3, we have elaborately optimized the ratio of di-tert-butyl decarbonate and HA-CD to find balance between the stability of the nanocluster and the instant pH-responsiveness. Compared with previous strategies, the present self-assembled nanocluster was able to sense the mild change in pH and respond instantly to dissociate into shotgun like small NPs.

DOX loading efficiency was quantified by UV/vis spectroscopy at 484 nm. The loading efficiency of DOX@HA-CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD were 17.02 ± 0.70% and 16.50 ± 0.28% (per 10 mg HA-CD), respectively. DOX was conjugated on HA-CD via pH-cleavable imine bond. Therefore, the acid-responsive drug release property was investigated in release media with different acidic values. As shown in Fig. S4, about 15% DOX was released at pH 7.4, while 33% was released at pH 6.5. Notably, over 60% DOX was released at pH 5.5 within 48 h, indicating a pH-responsive release profile of DOX. The in vitro release results suggested that BOC modification did not affect the release profile of DOX. We have scanned the UV spectrum of released DOX to verify its integrity. The released DOX retained the features of free DOX, which did not affect its toxicity (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro stability and hemocompatibility in serum
ce:section.ce:para: The stability results in 10% or 50% FBS was shown in Fig. S6. SA-DOX@HA-CD was well-dispersed after incubation in 10% or 50% FBS at different concentrations for over 24 h. These results indicated a good stability of SA-DOX@HA-CD in serum. Haemolysis less than 10% was considered as non-toxic in in vitro hemocompatibility experiment [37]. To calculate he haemolysis percentage, erythrocytes were incubated with different concentrations of SA-DOX@HA-CD ranging from 0.5 to 20 µg·ml−1 for 4 h. As shown in Fig. S7, no significant haemolysis was observed within the concentration range, indicating a good biocompatibility of SA-DOX@HA-CD. Cationic polymer such as PEI or PAMAM would result in complete erythrocytes lysis. Nevertheless, the haemolytic effect could be significantly alleviated by hyaluronic acid modification [38,39]. For this reason, the HA on the surface of SA-DOX@HA-CD contributed to its hemocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular internalization and translocation
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that HA can specifically bind to CD 44, which is overexpressed on various tumor cells [40,41]. Our previous study has showed that the DOX@HA-CD was capable of targeting to target 4T1 cells because HA residues remained on the surface of the products, as proved by the element analysis results. Smaller nanoparticles are more prone to be up-taken by cells than larger ones. SA-DOX @HA-CD could quickly transform into about 30-40 nm in acidic tumor microenvironment, endowing it with enhanced cellular up-take efficiency. This property was verified on 4T1 cells in stimulated acidic tumour microenvironment. As shown in the flow cytometry results in Fig. 4 A, a similar positive ratio was observed between DOX@CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD in pH 6.5, which was much higher than that of SA-DOX@HA-CD in pH 7.4, indicating the disassembled small nanoparticles facilitated cellular internalization. Notably, in physiological conditions, the nanocluster remained stable and were barely up-taken in cells, suggesting a tumor-targeted internalization. The fluorescence intensity was also observed by CLSM (Fig. 4B) and the results were well consistent with the result of flow cytometry.

Furthermore, we analysed the dynamic endocytosis process. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased in all groups from 1 h to 4 h. Obviously, DOX@CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD in pH 6.5 exhibited enhanced internalization in 4 h compared with SA-DOX@HA-CD in pH 7.4, further proving that the rapid particle size shrinkage facilitated cellular uptake. The semiquantitative results were presented in Supporting Information Fig. S8, demonstrating SA-DOX@HA-CD in pH 6.5 significantly enhanced cellular uptake at 4 h.

The result of intracellular translocation was shown in Fig. 5B-C. NPs from all groups distributed in lysosome at 4 h, where the imine bonds could be hydrolysed and release DOX due to the acid environment in lysosome, facilitating the translocation of DOX in the nucleus and its subsequent cytotoxic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro tumour spheroid penetration assay
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the difference between single-layer tumour cells and solid tumour, the penetration effect may not be accurately reflected by cellular uptake. In vitro tumour spheroids could provide tumour cells with a similar growth environment of tumour to imitate the in vivo status, which are more suitable for the investigation of tumour penetration of nano drug delivery systems. The penetrating capacity of the nanocluster was evaluated in 4T1 tumour spheroids by CLSM at different depth (Fig. 6 ). DOX@HA-CD exhibited potent deep penetration capacity due to its small particle sizes. Notably, SA-DOX@HA-CD in pH 6.5 showed similar deep penetration behavior and the fluorescent signals of DOX was observable at 70 µm. In comparison, SA-DOX@HA-CD in pH 7.4 demonstrated poor penetration due to the relatively large size. To further quantitatively compare the in vitro deep penetration, we conducted flow cytometry analysis. after treatment with different formulations for 6 h, the tumor spheroids were treated with Accumax Cell Dissociation Solution to obtain single cell suspension. As shown in Fig. S9, SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 6.5 exhibited significantly higher cellular internalization than SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 7.4. Compared with small-sized DOX@HA-CD, the fluorescence of SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 6.5 was slightly lower, however, the difference was non-significant. This phenomenon further proved that SA-DOX@HA-CD could swiftly respond to the acid microenvironment and disassemble into smaller NPs to penetrate to deep regions in tumor spheroid, thus overcoming the extracelluar matrix barrier (ECM) in tumor.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of the nanocluster in 4T1 cells under different pH, MTT assay was performed (Fig. S10). A concentration-dependent manner was observed in the viability of 4T1 cells viability in all treated groups. A weak cytotoxicity was observed when exposed to 0.01 µg·mL−1 DOX (cell viability > 90%) and the cell viability reduced to 40% at DOX concentration 10 µg·mL−1 after incubation for 24 h. Notably, owing to the rapid size shrinkage in simulated acidic tumor microenvironment, SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 6.5 induced higher cytotoxicity in 4T1 cells than that at pH 7.4 with the same DOX concentration. Free doxorubicin group exhibited higher toxicity than its formulated forms, probably due to its free diffusion across the cell membrane.

We further have conducted in vitro apoptosis analysis to explore the apoptotic inducing effect on 4T1 cells. As shown in Fig. S11, free DOX induced the highest percentage of apoptosis. Compared with SA-DOX@HA-CD at physiological pH, the disassembled SA-DOX@HA-CD at pH 6.5 exhibited higher apoptosis, probably owing to the enhanced cellular uptake of smaller nanoparticles. This result is in consistency with the cytotoxicity and in vivo caspase-3 assay. Nevertheless, for in vivo tumor targeting, nanoparticle-based drug carrier prolonged the circulation of DOX and enhanced the tumor accumulation, outperforming free DOX in inhibiting tumor growth. In addition, self-disassembled nanocluster further promoted the cellular uptake and deep penetration process, further enhancing the anti-tumor effect.

To determine the tumor targeting efficiency of SA-DOX@HA-CD, an in vivo distribution assay was performed using DOX as a fluorescent probe. The nano formulations or free DOX were intravenously injected into the orthotopical 4T1 tumour-bearing BALB/c mice via the tail vein. The tumours and major organs were harvested and then examined by fluorescence imaging. As shown in Fig. 7 A, due to the rapid elimination of free DOX, weak fluorescence was observed at 1 h and very little fluorescence remained after 8 h. Intravenously administered DOX@HA-CD also demonstrated poor tumor accumulation, probably because of the quick elimination of small NPs. Among all the groups, the strongest fluorescent intensity was observed in SA-DOX@HA-CD treated mice, the fluorescence of which peaked at 4 h and then gradually decreased. This result could be attributed to the EPR effect of SA-DOX@HA-CD, which enhanced the tumor retention and accumulation. The semi-quantitative analysis is consistent with the results of fluorescence imaging (Fig. 7B). Tumor and major organs were collected for frozen sections. The fluorescence imaging and semi-quantitative results indicated that SA-DOX@HA-CD significantly facilitated the accumulation of DOX (Fig. 7C, Fig. S12). This observation might be attributed to the active tumour targeting mediated by HA, enhanced accumulation and rapid size shrinkage in the TME.

Fluorescent images of isolated organs at 4 h and 24 h time point as shown in Fig. S13. Due to the high affinity to myocardium, DOX exhibited obvious distribution in heart, and the formulation of nanomedicine decreased the accumulation in heart. The section of heart further confirmed this observation (Fig. S14). Since the endocytic hyaluronan receptor existed in liver sinusoidal endothelial cells, both DOX@HA-CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD showed accumulation in liver.

According to previous literatures, the size, shape, charge and surface properties may strongly affect the blood circulation and tumor penetration behavior of nanoparticles. Among these factors, the size exhibited the greatest influence. In general, smaller nanoparticles usually exhibited good tumor penetration and could overcome the high interstitial fluid pressure. However, as shown in Fig. 7A, smaller nanoparticles were easily eliminated and showed poor tumor retention. While larger nanoparticles can make up this shortcoming. Therefore, various size changeable drug delivery systems have been designed using the acid or enzyme-rich properties of TME. In the present manuscript, the rapid self-dissassemble nanoparticles demonstrated sensitive pH-triggered size shrinkage, which consequently enhanced the tumor retention and accumulation of DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SA-DOX@HA-CD elevated antitumor effects in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous literatures, chemotherapeutics penetrating into the core region of tumor will lead to enhance antitumor effect, where is the source of tumor recurrence, metastasis and drug resistance [42–44]. To evaluate the therapeutic efficiency of SA-DOX@HA-CD, orthotopic 4T1 cell tumour models was established. Mice was treated intravenously with PBS, free DOX, DOX@HA-CD or SA-DOX@HA-CD, respectively. The antitumor effects were illustrated in Fig. 8 A-C. Compared with PBS treated tumor group, mice treated with DOX in either free or formulation form showed inhibited tumour growth to different extents. The tumor volume and tumor weight in SA-DOX@HA-CD treated group were significantly lower than that in DOX@HA-CD group, indicating the deep penetration of disassembled nanoparticle elevated the antitumor effect of chemotherapy. The best antitumor effect was observed in SA-DOX@HA-CD treated mice with relative tumour growth inhibition of 78.53%. In comparison, the relative tumour growth inhibition was 56.90% and 47.63% in DOX@HA-CD and free DOX group, respectively (Fig. 8A-C). To synchronously monitor the toxicity, the body weight was measured every other day during treatment (Fig. 8D). An obvious body weight loss of about 15-17% was observed in the free DOX group, while both DOX@HA-CD and SA-DOX@HA-CD group did not demonstrate obvious body weight change. The results indicated that the formulation of HA-CD could effectively reduce the systemic toxicity of DOX. To sum up, the SA-DOX@HA-CD effectively inhibited tumour growth in 4T1 tumour models, which should be attributed to the enhanced tumour accumulation and swift TME responsive shrinkage into small nanoparticles. ki-67 is protein related to nuclear proliferation, which is closely related to the degree of differentiation, invasion, metastasis and prognosis in various malignant tumours including breast cancer [45]. As one of the most important apoptosis executors in the caspase family, caspase-3 is frequently used as an indicator to evaluate apoptosis level. Based on the immunohistochemical sections shown in Fig. 8E, it could be observed that a relatively low ki-67 level and high expression of caspase-3 were observed in SA-DOX@HA-CD treated mice, indicating that SA-DOX@HA-CD exhibited the best antitumor efficiency in the orthotopic 4T1 cell tumour models among all groups. The potential tissue toxicity was investigated by H&E-stained sections histopathology analysis. As shown in Fig. S15, no obvious pathological changes were seen in the both SA-DOX@HA-CD and DOX@HA-CD treatment group.

The blood chemistry analysis was performed to further determine the toxicity of the self-assembled nanocluster. Creatine kinase (CK), is an important index to evaluate cardiotoxicity. The AST, ALT, BUN and Cre are important indicators of liver and kidney functions. For blood biochemical indexes (Fig. S16), the AST, BUN, ALT and Cre levels in experimental groups were non-significant compared with PBS treated group. Notably, free DOX significantly increased the CK levels in comparison with PBS and SA-DOX@HA-CD, which can be explained by the cardiotoxicity of free DOX. These results indicate that the self-assembled SA-DOX@HA-CD was a biocompatible drug delivery system with minimum adverse effects on heart, kidney or liver at the indicated dosage. This may be owing to the elevated tumor targeting and accumulation mediated by HA as well as the EPR effects. More importantly, the self-assembled ability endowed the nanoparticle with better EPR effect. The rapid and accurate disassemble in acidic TME further facilitated the deep penetration and accumulation of DOX in tumor sites.

In conclusion, this work presented an instant acid responsive size-shrinkable drug delivery system. SA-DOX@HA-CD was constructed by introducing hydrophobic group tert-butoxycarbonyl into DOX@HA-CD, forming a raspberry-like nanocluster in physiological pH conditions and exhibiting good hemocompatibility and stability. While in the acidic tumor micronenvironment, due to the change of charge and hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity, the aggregated SA-DOX@HA-CD instantly dissociated into shotgun bomb-type small-sized monomer nanoparticles DOX@HA-CD. Under simulated tumor microenvironment pH, the thorough size-shrinkage manner led to enhanced cellular uptake and deep penetration in 3D tumour spheroid model. Furthermore, owing to the elevated tumor accumulation and penetration effect, SA-DOX@HA-CD showed higher tumor targeting efficiency compared with small-sized DOX@HA-CD and free DOX in orthotopic 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model. Consequently, the rapid size-shrinkable nanocluster SA-DOX@HA-CD yielded enhance anti-tumor effects and reduced the heart toxicity of DOX. This pH-responsive, self-assembled nanocluster provides a safe and efficient alternative for balancing tumor accumulation and deep penetration.
